context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I need help figuring out this 700–800 word reflection on pro vs anti-development. idk how to frame the argument yet, esp the part about whether anti-development is nihilist/utopian or if the whole divide is too binary.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that tension is actually at the heart of the prompt. Before we talk framing, let me ask you: in your own words, what do you think 'anti-development' actually means? What are its core claims?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very start of the process. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment, I should ask them a focused opening question that activates their prior thinking on the core concept. The Socratic approach here means starting with what they already know or think, before moving to framing. I'll keep it short and targeted.

exchanges.student: I think it’s basically criticizing mainstream development, not just being “against improvement” lol. like maybe it says development can be top-down, Western, growth-obsessed, and harmful even when it claims to help.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point. So if anti-development is a critique rather than just rejection — does that make it nihilist, utopian, or something else? What do you think those two labels would actually mean when applied to a development theory?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of anti-development as a critique of mainstream/Western development models. This is a good foundation. Now I should push them to think more precisely about the utopian/nihilist distinction the prompt raises, since that's where they said they were stuck. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to get them to think through what those two labels actually mean in this context.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m thinking neither, or like partly both depending on the author. “nihilist” would mean rejecting development w no workable alternative, and “utopian” would mean imagining totally different social relations that sound good but may be hard to implement.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now here's a follow-up: if some anti-development thinkers are neither fully nihilist nor fully utopian, what does that suggest about the pro vs. anti divide itself? Is it a useful distinction at all?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and arriving at a nuanced position independently. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without validating it as a complete answer, and push them to connect this insight to the prompt's next challenge: the 'too Manichean' question. This will help them see that their 'neither/both' intuition actually leads naturally into questioning the pro/anti binary itself.

exchanges.student: ya that makes me think the divide is kinda too Manichean. ppl can reject a specific model of development but still want welfare, justice, participation, or planning in some form.
exchanges.tutor: You're building a coherent argument there. Now, the prompt also asks about historical context — why did certain anti-development arguments emerge at specific times? What do you know about when these critiques peaked, and what was happening in the world then?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has now connected two key ideas: the nuanced nature of anti-development and the limitations of the binary. To keep moving toward a complete reflection, I should now push them toward the historical context question in the prompt, which they haven't touched yet. This is an important component of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit fuzzy on the timeline tbh. I guess one wave came after postwar modernization failed a lot of places, and maybe another after neoliberal reforms/structural adjustment made inequality worse?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with two waves. Can you think of any specific thinkers, movements, or terms associated with each of those moments? Even partial examples would help you anchor the argument historically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of the two waves but is fuzzy on details. I should push them to think more precisely rather than filling in the gaps for them. A targeted question about what specific intellectual or political movements emerged in those moments will help them connect history to theory.

exchanges.student: maybe dependency theory for the earlier critique? and later post-development people like Escobar? I’m not fully sure who fits where tho.
exchanges.tutor: Those are relevant names. Rather than worrying about who fits exactly where, think about this: what was happening politically and economically that made those critiques feel urgent or necessary at those specific moments? What were they responding to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying key figures and movements. Rather than confirming or correcting the placement, I should push them to think about what connects these thinkers to their historical moments — this will help them build the historical argument the prompt requires rather than just listing names.

exchanges.student: I think they were responding to big promises not matching reality—states planning a lot but still getting poverty/debt, then later market reforms getting sold as efficiency but causing cuts and social harm. so anti-development arguments felt urgent when dominant models lost legitimacy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong historical argument. Now the prompt asks something very practical: can someone who is anti-development still plan? How would you answer that, given everything you've just worked out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made a strong connection between historical context and the rise of anti-development critiques. This is a key insight for their paper. They've now covered most of the prompt's questions. I should push them toward the last major piece they haven't addressed: can one be anti-development and still plan? This connects their historical/theoretical work back to planning practice, which is central to the course.

exchanges.student: I’d say yes, but they’d be against certain kinds of planning, not all planning. like they might reject technocratic/top-down planning and still support participatory, local, or justice-centered planning.
exchanges.tutor: You now have a solid set of ideas covering most of the prompt. I'd say it's time to stop discussing and start drafting. Can you try writing an opening paragraph that introduces your central argument about the pro/anti divide?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through most of the key components of the prompt: the nihilist/utopian distinction, the Manichean binary, historical context, and the planning question. They have enough material to start writing. Following the rule about sensing redundancy and pushing toward written output after sufficient conceptual development, I should now encourage them to start drafting rather than continuing to discuss. I should not write anything for them.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: “Anti-development arguments should not be dismissed as simply nihilist or utopian. Many are best understood as historically grounded critiques of dominant development models, especially when those models became coercive, unequal, or ineffective. In that sense, the divide between pro- and anti-development is often too simplistic, because critics of development may still support planning aimed at justice, participation, and local autonomy.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening with a clear argumentative thread. One thing I notice: you developed a really compelling historical argument in our conversation — about why these critiques emerged when dominant models lost legitimacy — but it's not in this paragraph yet. How might you weave that in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid opening paragraph that captures their core argument. It touches on the nihilist/utopian question, the Manichean divide, and hints at alternative planning. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading, and push them to think about what's missing or could be stronger — particularly the historical context argument they developed well in conversation but hasn't made it into the paragraph yet.

exchanges.student: maybe I add that anti-development rises in moments of crisis, when state-led modernization or neoliberal reform stop looking credible? I’m not sure how to fit that in smoothly tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right idea to add. But figuring out how to fit it in smoothly is your job as the writer — I can't help with phrasing or structure. Give it a try and show me what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to help them with the writing/language, which is something I must refuse per the rules. I should redirect them clearly but constructively — they need to do the writing themselves. I can affirm the idea is right and push them to attempt it.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic dialogue throughout all ten turns. Key ideas were consistently drawn from the student rather than supplied by the tutor. The tutor built progressively on each student response, maintained appropriate scaffolding, kept responses concise and focused, and explicitly declined to assist with phrasing in the final turn — reinforcing the student's ownership of the writing. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — all key ideas (nihilism/utopianism framing, Manichean divide, historical waves, planning-while-anti-development) were elicited through questioning. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress at each turn and used diagnostic questions before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback in Turn 10 (noting the historical argument was missing from the draft paragraph) rather than vague praise. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misframing, and no off-topic drift. Communication was bite-sized and supportive throughout, with no grading language used.
